coredata.dc:title: Large Stokes-shift AIE fluorescent materials for high-performance luminescent solar concentrators
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Wenwen
Li, Wenjing
Cao, Mengyan
Liu, Ruiyuan
Zhao, Xiujian
Gong, Xiao
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have showed great interests since they can realize building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). To date, a variety of luminophores have been developed to fabricate LSCs. In this paper, we designed and prepared aggregation-induced emission (AIE) fluorescent organic nanoparticles (TPFE-Rho), and reported that TPFE-Rho nanoparticles could be used as the efficient fluorescent materials for LSC devices since they show good photostability, large Stokes shift, and low toxicity. The high-performance TPFE-Rho/PMMA slab based LSCs with a power conversion efficiency of 3.55% could be obtained when the concentration of TPFE-Rho nanoparticles within the PMMA matrix was 0.08 wt%. Our results reveal that TPFE-Rho/PMMA slab based LSCs exhibit high performance and can realize the semi-transparent photovoltaic windows.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Luminescent solar concentrators
Luminophores
Aggregation-induced emission

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Chemical structure of TPFE-Rho. (b) Typical SEM image of TPFE-Rho nanoparticle. (c) Schematic illustration of LSCs made of PMMA polymer matrix comprising TPFE-Rho. (d) Photograph of a PMMA slab based LSC comprising 0.06 wt% TPFE-Rho under 365 nm UV illumination (dimensions: 1.8 cm × 1.8 cm × 0.5 cm). It is apparent that the emitted light is wave-guided to the slab edges through total internal reflection.

UV–vis absorption (black lines) and photoluminescence (red lines) spectra of TPFE-Rho in water/THF mixture (water fraction 70%). Insets: the photograph of TPFE-Rho in water/THF mixture under daylight (left) and under 365 nm UV illumination (right). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a). (b). Absorption and photoluminescence spectra of TPFE-Rho/PMMA polymer matrix based LSCs with 0.25 cm and 0.5 cm thickness respectively, containing different concentrations of TPFE-Rho (0.02 wt%, 0.04 wt%, 0.06 wt%, 0.08 wt% and 0.10 wt% respectively) under 470 nm excitation. (c). Normalized photoluminescence spectra of TPFE-Rho/PMMA polymer matrix based LSCs with the 0.5 cm thickness under 470 nm excitation.
Room-temperature photoluminescence decays of TPFE-Rho in the water/THF mixture and TPFE-Rho/PMMA polymer matrix with different concentrations (excitation at 470 nm).
J-V curves of the LSCs doped TPFE-Rho with different concentrations and thicknesses (left: 0.25 cm, right: 0.5 cm).

head.ce:title: Large Stokes-shift AIE fluorescent materials for high-performance luminescent solar concentrators

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, Southern Medical University, Guangzhou, 510515, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have showed great interests since they can realize building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). To date, a variety of luminophores have been developed to fabricate LSCs. In this paper, we designed and prepared aggregation-induced emission (AIE) fluorescent organic nanoparticles (TPFE-Rho), and reported that TPFE-Rho nanoparticles could be used as the efficient fluorescent materials for LSC devices since they show good photostability, large Stokes shift, and low toxicity. The high-performance TPFE-Rho/PMMA slab based LSCs with a power conversion efficiency of 3.55% could be obtained when the concentration of TPFE-Rho nanoparticles within the PMMA matrix was 0.08 wt%. Our results reveal that TPFE-Rho/PMMA slab based LSCs exhibit high performance and can realize the semi-transparent photovoltaic windows.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are a new type of solar energy utilization devices, which consist of transparent or semitransparent matrix materials and fluorescent active substances [1–5]. When luminophores are evenly dispersed in the transparent matrix, they can absorb the incident light and emit the fluorescence photon which can be absorbed by the solar cell [6–8]. Once a solar cell is installed on the side of a fluorescent solar collector, the process of converting light energy into electric energy can be realized [9–11]. Compared with the traditional solar concentrating device, LSCs show many advantages: first, because of flat-panel structure they can receive radiation in any direction of the solar light without affecting the photoelectric conversion efficiency [12,13]; second, they only input the collected fluorescence photon into the side of the device, so they reduce the photon energy, decreasing the heat effect of the spotlight effectively; furthermore, LSCs only need side-mounted solar cells which can significantly reduce the number and size of solar cells, and cut the cost of the entire photoelectric conversion [14–16]. Therefore, the device is not only suitable for solar roof, glass curtain wall, but also can be made into a solar window power generation system [17–19]. LSCs can reduce building energy consumption, realize building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV) application, and show a broad application prospect.

Recently, the range of luminescent materials has been greatly broadened, and many luminophores have emerged such as metal complexes [3,20], organic dyes [21–23] and quantum dots [8,24–30]. However, the self-absorption and fluorescence quenching [31] are common problems that dramatically limit the application of LSCs [1,32]. Therefore, it is a great challenge to find suitable fluorescent substances with large Stokes shift, long lifetime and excellent photostability. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) fluorescent organic nanoparticles are designed and synthesized initially for sensing, bio-imaging and optoelectronic applications [12,33–37]. Very recently, some efforts have been devoted to the development of fabricating LSCs using AIE materials [12]. Because AIE luminogens (AIEgens) have good photostability, large Stokes shift, and low toxicity, we try to use AIEgens to fabricate high-performance LSCs based on our very recently developed AIEgens [38].

AIEgens are non-emissive when dissolved in good solvents. However they become highly luminescent in poor solvents or in a solid state [39–41] since a prototypical AIE molecule generally has several rotatable aromatic substituents, which are connected to a conjugate center. In a solution, the free rotation of these aromatic substituents dissipates the excited state energy, becoming a non-radiative decay pathway, and resulting in a weak fluorescence. However, in the state of aggregation or solid, molecular motion is blocked, driving the molecule to decay radiatively, thus enhancing the fluorescence [42–45]. In this study, we prepared AIE-active fluorescent organic nanoparticles (TPFE-Rho nanoparticles), and reported for the first time that TPFE-Rho could be employed as phosphors to be embedded in PMMA polymer matrix for high-performance LSC fabrication.

In the study, a novel AIE fluorophore was synthesized and utilized, and the fluorescence quantum yield of TPFE-Rho was 34.88%. The chemical structure of TPFE-Rho is showed in Fig. 1 a. SEM technique was employed to obtain the information about the size and shape of the TPFE-Rho nanoparticle. As shown in Fig. 1b, it can be clearly observed that the TPFE-Rho nanoparticle exhibited nearly spherical shape which had a size of around 110 nm. Fig. 1c is a schematic illustration of the LSC made of a polymer matrix comprising TPFE-Rho nanoparticles. There is only one photovoltaic cell mounted on the side of the LSC, and the remaining edges of the LSC are covered with mirrors. When light is incident from the top of the LSC, TPFE-Rho nanoparticles absorb partly direct and diffuse sunlight radiation and re-emit them at higher wavelengths. Part of the emission falls within the escape cone, and others are guided to the photovoltaic cell by total internal reflection. Fig. 1d shows the photograph of an LSC comprising 0.06 wt% TPFE-Rho nanoparticles dispersed in PMMA polymer matrix. In our case, we fabricated two kinds of LSCs with different thicknesses (1.8 × 1.8 × 0.5 cm3 and 1.8 × 1.8 × 0.25 cm3) containing different TPFE-Rho nanoparticle contents (wt%), respectively. So far, a number of polymers have been used as matrix materials for LSCs (e.g., poly (lauryl methacrylate) (PLMA), polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) and polycarbonate (PC)) [19,33,45–47]. Compared with other polymer matrix materials [48–51], PMMA has advantages including cheap, high refractive index, good optical properties and mechanical properties, and has been widely used in windows. Therefore, we chose PMMA as the polymer matrix materials.

The TPFE-Rho showed an absorption maximum at 470 nm (Fig. 2 ), and a fluorescence peak at 597 nm in water/THF mixture (water fraction 70%). The fluorescence intensity of TPFE-Rho in THF was very weak. However, when a large amount of water was added to THF solution, the fluorescence was enhanced along with red-emitting fluorophore under 365 nm UV illumination. This phenomenon can be explained by the aggregation of TPFE-Rho nanoparticles, thus activating the AIE process and leading to the formation of high emission. The Stokes shift is about 127 nm which is noteworthy and promising for the application of TPFE-Rho nanoparticles in LSCs.

Fig. 3 a–b displays the absorption and photoluminescence spectra of TPFE-Rho/PMMA polymer matrix based LSCs with the thickness of 0.25 cm and 0.5 cm, respectively, containing different TPFE-Rho nanoparticle concentrations (0.02 wt% - 0.10 wt%) under 470 nm excitation. By analysis of the TPFE-Rho absorption with water/THF mixture and PMMA polymer matrix, we can see that there is no obvious difference. It showed that TPFE-Rho had a broad absorption band in the range of 400–550 nm and a maximum intensity at around 470 nm. Fluorescence emission spectra of TPFE-Rho/PMMA with the thickness of 0.25 cm containing different concentrations (0.02 wt%-0.10 wt%) were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increase of concentrations, which should be ascribed to the fact that more TPFE-Rho nanoparticles are doped in the LSCs. However, the fluorescence intensity of the TPFE-Rho/PMMA (thickness: 0.5 cm) increased with the increase of concentrations, reached the maximum at 0.08 wt%, and then decreased with the increase of the concentrations (Fig. 3b). It is supposed that fluorescence quenching caused by aggregation can also occur in AIE systems under higher concentration. In addition, the fluorescence peaking was at around 570 nm, and the Stokes shift was about 100 nm. Compared with TPFE-Rho in the water/THF mixture, the fluorescence exhibited slight blue-shift. The phenomenon of red-shift of the emission band is largely due to the presence of water in THF solution that leads to TPFE-Rho aggregation, changing the wavelength of the luminescence compared with TPFE-Rho dispersed in PMMA polymer matrix. Fig. 3c shows the normalized photoluminescence spectra of LSCs with the 0.5 cm thickness had a slight red-shift under 470 nm excitation.

According to Fig. 4 , the PL decays of the emissions from TPFE-Rho/PMMA polymer matrix with different concentrations had almost the identical lifetime of ~4.47 ns, which means the similar luminescent process. The PL lifetime of the TPFE-Rho in water/THF mixture was ~4.03 ns which is slightly shorter than that of TPFE-Rho in PMMA polymer matrix, indicating there is almost no effect on the PL dynamics of TPFE-Rho before (in solutions) and after they are embedded into the polymer matrix.

The photovoltaic performance of solar cells and the LSC devices was tested under the condition of simulating sunlight, and the characteristic J-V curves were drawn. In order to make the test results comparable, according to the solar photovoltaic energy systems standardization technical committee (IEC-TC82), the test temperature was 25 ± 2 °C, and the light irradiation was at 1000 W/m2 under AM 1.5G solar. Following parameters were measured and considered [4,52–54].

The optical conversion efficiency refers to the ratio of the power coming out of the slab edge of the LSC (P
out ) to the power coming in (P
in ) through the top surface.
(1)
η
o p t
=
P
o u t
/
P
i n
which can be simply defined as
(2)
η
o p t
=
I
L S C
I
S C
× G

The optical efficiency (η
opt ) generally determines the performance of an LSC, which is determined by the lumiphore and the matrix. The I
LSC is the short circuit current from the PV cell attached to the LSC slab, and I
sc is the short circuit current measured from the same solar cell under direct illumination with the same light source. The geometric gain G is a ratio of the top surface area (A
top ) to the edge surface area (A
edge ) of the LSC slab, depending on the dimensions of the LSC slab.
(3)
G =
A
t o p
/
A
e d g e

Another parameter used to define the performance of an LSC device is the overall power conversion efficiency η defined as
(4)
η =
P
m a x
P
i n
=
I
s c
· F F ·
V
o c
P
i n
· A
where I
sc is the short circuit current, FF is the fill factor, V
oc is the open circuit voltage, P
in is the irradiation intensity and A is the area of the LSC top surface. In addition, the values of J sc, V oc, FF and C (the effective concentration factor) were obtained from the photocurrent density–voltage (J–V) curves.

Fig. 5 shows J-V curves of the LSCs doped with TPFE-Rho with different concentrations and thicknesses (left: 0.25 cm, right: 0.5 cm). As shown in Fig. 5 (left), with the increase of the concentration of TPFE-Rho, the photocurrent density also increased. Table S1 listed the photovoltaic parameters such as V
oc , J
sc , G, η
opt and η obtained in the current density and voltage test. With the increase of TPFE-Rho concentration, η also increased. When the concentration of TPFE-Rho reached 0.1 wt%, the value of η could reach up to 3.13% and got maximum, which is consistent with the result of fluorescence emission spectrum. According to Fig. 5 (right) and Table S2, when the thickness changed to 0.5 cm, the value of η first increased with the increase of the concentration of TPFE-Rho, and then decreased a little. This may be due to the fact that the doping amount of fluorescent materials could not increase indefinitely. When the doping amount reaches a limit value and the TPFE-Rho concentration increases further, the fluorescence intensity decreases instead, that is, fluorescence quenching occurs. When the concentration of TPFE-Rho reached 0.08 wt%, the maximum value of η could reach up to 3.55%. The optimal values of V
oc , J
sc , and η
opt could reach up to 0.52 V, 9.97 mA/cm2, 4.09, respectively. Based on this, it has been demonstrated that the TPFE-Rho nanoparticles are able to be effective luminophores for high-performance LSC fabrication.

In this work, we designed and prepared AIE fluorescent organic nanoparticles (TPFE-Rho nanoparticles). Because of their good photostability, large Stokes shift, and low toxicity, we first reported that TPFE-Rho could be as the fluorescent materials for LSC devices. We performed current-voltage measurements on LSCs with two thicknesses and different concentrations of TPFE-Rho nanoparticles. The optimal values of J sc, V oc, η opt and η can reach up to 9.97 mA/cm2, 0.52 V, 4.09%, and 3.55%, respectively. These data illustrate that the TPFE-Rho nanoparticles are expected to be used in LSC fabrication, realizing the semi-transparent photovoltaic windows.
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098, 21104069), Open Foundation of the State Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials for Architectures at WUT (No. SYSJJ2018-04), and the Excellent Dissertation Cultivation Funds of Wuhan University of Technology (2017-YS-007). W. Li acknowledges the Independent Innovation Foundation of Wuhan University of Technology (195259002) for the financial support of this research. M. Cao acknowledges the National Students’ platform for innovation and entrepreneurship training program (20181049721002) for the financial support of this research.
